exemplar.title: To what extent did Primark's environmental strategy 'Primark Cares' help improve its brand image?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent did Primark's environmental strategy 'Primark Cares' help improve its brand image?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear definition and repeated reference to sustainability throughout the investigation
Good anchoring of Primark-specific data (store numbers, revenue figures, director quotes) to the key concept
weaknesses: Occasional drift into descriptive listing of targets without critical interrogation
Integration of the concept is strong in most sections but not fully sustained in every paragraph
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the submission shows a strong grasp of sustainability and links it well to Primark, the analysis sometimes reverts to description and general commentary. This performance best fits band 3 (âgood understanding with clear analytical thinkingâ), rather than band 4 or 5, since integration is not seamless in every part of the IA.

strengths: Exactly five supporting documents meet the 3â5 requirement
All sources are directly relevant to Primarkâs sustainability strategy
weaknesses: Depth is limited by reliance on secondary journalistic pieces without methodological detail
Lacks range of rigorous academic or industry studies to deepen the evidence base
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate has provided a sufficient number of relevant documents (band 3: ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ), but to reach the top band they would need more varied, peer-reviewed or primary-data sources to enhance depth and credibility.

strengths: Selection of three recognized frameworks (Triple Bottom Line, CSR, Circular Model) shows awareness of key theories
weaknesses: Applications are largely descriptive and occasionally repetitive
Relevance of some theory sections to the research question is not always clear
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student selects appropriate tools, their application remains superficial in places and at times the link back to the research question is only implied. This aligns with band 2 (âsome tools selectedâ with âsuperficial relevanceâ), rather than higher bands requiring clear, effective application.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Uses Primark data (store count, revenue, carbon-reduction targets) to support points
Balances positives and negatives (e.g., loyalty vs. overproduction) in parts of the analysis
weaknesses: Data selection is basic and lacks breadth or triangulation
Overall evaluation remains at an adequate, rather than a fully critical or sustained, level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis goes beyond mere description but is not sufficiently broad or sustained to warrant band 3 or higher. It fits band 2: âadequate selection and use of dataâ with âsome analysis,â rather than the integrated, mostly effective analysis of higher bands.

strengths: Conclusions are fully consistent with the evidence presented
Explicitly answers the research question and acknowledges caveats
weaknesses: Lacks precise quantification of âto what extentâ brand image improved
Some repetition of earlier points makes the conclusion slightly redundant
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion synthesizes the findings coherently and directly addresses the research question, fitting the top band (band 3). Minor issues with redundancy and lack of metric precision prevent an exceptional distinction.

strengths: Logical Table of Contents and clear use of headings
weaknesses: Several section endings are incomplete or abrupt
Introduction ends mid-sentence, indicating structural gaps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall structure is present and generally coherent, but noticeable lapses (incomplete thoughts, abrupt section breaks) mean it does not sustain an appropriate structure throughout. This corresponds to band 1â2; the work most closely matches band 2 given the overall navigability, despite some inconsistencies.

strengths: All required IA elements are present: title page, word count, TOC, citations, bibliography
weaknesses: Formatting of the Table of Contents could be improved (dot leaders)
Minor inconsistencies in heading styles across sections
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission includes every required element, fitting band 2 (âall required elements includedâ). A few formatting inconsistencies prevent a lower score, but overall the presentation fully meets expectations.

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA contains a clear table of contents, logical headings, and a coherent flow from introduction to conclusion. Paragraphing is consistent, so an **appropriate structure is maintained throughout**, warranting the highest mark available for this strand (2).

All sources are directly relevant (Primark website, Statista revenue data, fast-fashion environmental articles). They provide sufficient evidence for each analytical point, but depth is limited: figures are quoted without methodological detail, and most sources are secondary journalistic pieces rather than industry or academic studies. Hence the description **ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ** fits better than the top-band requirement for a wide range of in-depth ideas.

Title page, word count, table of contents, in-text citations and bibliography are all present. No essential element appears to be missing, so the work meets the descriptor **âall required elements included.â**
Five supporting documents are listed, meeting the 3-5 requirement exactly. The quantity criterion is therefore satisfied at the 3-mark level.

Each tool is applied to Primark with clear links to brand image (e.g. revenue growth connected to TBL âProfitâ). Nevertheless, several applications remain largely descriptive and occasionally repeat the same point (e.g. âimproved trustâ). The relevance is clear but the effectiveness is uneven, corresponding to the descriptor: **ârelevance not always clear but adequately applied.â**

Data include store count, revenue changes, percentage carbon-reduction goals and supply-chain targets. These figures are used to illustrate points but are limited in breadth and critical depth. Selection and use are therefore **adequate** rather than âsufficientâ or âeffectiveâ, justifying 3 marks.

The analysis moves beyond description by weighing benefits (improved loyalty, revenue) against weaknesses (over-production, cotton drawbacks). Links between evidence and evaluation are mostly clear, showing **mostly effective analysis with some integration**. The integration is not sustained enough for the highest band but clearly surpasses limited or superficial analysis.

The research questionââTo what extent didâ¦improve brand image?ââis explicitly answered in the conclusion: Primarkâs image improved âsignificantlyâ albeit with caveats. This direct response satisfies the top band for the strand.

The student selects three well-chosen toolsâTriple Bottom Line, CSR, and Circular Business Modelâeach directly pertinent to evaluating environmental strategy and image. This demonstrates an **effective, rather than merely adequate, selection** and covers social, environmental, and operational dimensions, satisfying the 4-mark description.

The conclusion restates findings and is consistent with the evidence cited (revenue rise, transparency gains, remaining over-production issue). There are no statements that contradict earlier data, meeting the descriptor for full consistency.

The report consistently anchors ideas to Primark: store numbers, revenue figures, specific 2030 targets, and quotations from the sustainability director. These references show a clear relationship between theory and the actual organisation. Integration is evident in most but not every paragraph â at times the narrative drifts into general fast-fashion commentary. Thus the quality matches **âstrong analysis with partial integration throughoutâ** rather than the fully seamless integration required for 5 marks.

The student explicitly chooses sustainability as the key concept, defines it in the introduction and then returns to it in every major section (Triple Bottom Line â Planet/People/Profit; CSR; Circular Business Model). Sustainability is not treated as an isolated add-on but used as the lens for judging Primarkâs strategy and brand image. However, the discussion sometimes slips into descriptive commentary (e.g. listing targets without critiquing feasibility) so the analysis is only partially, not fully, integrated. This aligns with the descriptor for 4 marks: **âstrong understanding with partial integration throughout.â**

annotations.comment: Critique of Primarkâs overproduction under âPlanetâ shows critical thinking. Expanding this section with quantitative estimates of waste would strengthen evaluation.
The âPeopleâ subsection explains ethical sourcing well but lacks supporting data on worker outcomes or consumer perceptions. Incorporate survey or impact statistics.
Opening discussion of transitions to sustainable resources is clear. To advance analysis, compare Primarkâs progress to industry benchmarks.
Profit analysis uses revenue growth effectively. However, attributing all growth to sustainability overlooks other factors; consider alternative explanations.
Labeling the âProfitâ header distinctly helps structure the analysis. Consistent use of subheadings aids reader navigation.
The CSR environmental section articulates commitments well but would benefit from discussing stakeholder risks, such as allegations of greenwashing.
Ethical Responsibility analysis provides good context on labour practices, but the revenue figure is repeated from earlier. Avoid redundancy by integrating fresh evidence.
Circular Business Model is defined clearly, but lacks a concrete Primark example. Incorporate a case such as a specific take-back program to illustrate application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The Circular Supply Model section balances positives and negatives effectively, demonstrating mostly effective analysis.
Discussion of renewable cottonâs limitations is insightful. To deepen analysis, reference lifecycle assessment data on cotton farming impacts.
The Resource Recovery Model section ends abruptly and should be completed. A full explanation of how product life extension influences brand perception is needed.

The title page clearly states the key concept âSustainabilityâ, providing a solid anchor for the entire investigation. This explicit identification aligns with a strong integration of the key concept.
The candidate information and word count are presented correctly, showing adherence to required formatting and inclusion of all elements.
Table of Contents headings are organized logically, reflecting an appropriate structure throughout the document.
The contents table spans several pages, but page references appear accurate. Consider adding dot leaders to improve readability and navigation.
The opening paragraph provides good contextual data about Primark, but could integrate a brief critique of fast fashionâs broader impacts rather than solely descriptive background.
The introduction ends mid-sentence (âand the Circularâ), indicating an incomplete thought. Ensure full sentences to maintain clarity and structural coherence.

The research question is clear but could be refined to specify mechanisms by which âPrimark Caresâ influenced brand image rather than the broader phrase âpublicity of its environmental movements.â
The definition of the Triple Bottom Line is accurate, but the discussion remains somewhat generic. Tie definitions directly to Primarkâs data to deepen application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The note on potential bias in secondary sources is perceptive. Acknowledge how Primarkâs corporate narrative might skew analysis.
Listing specific 2030 sustainability targets under âPlanetâ is effective use of data. Consider evaluating the feasibility and potential barriers to achieving these targets.
The conclusion restates major findings but should more explicitly quantify âto what extentâ Primarkâs image improved, linking back to specific metrics.
The final paragraph reiterates earlier analysis. Consider consolidating to avoid redundancy and maintain concise conclusions.
The closing bibliography lists relevant sources, but adding peer-reviewed academic studies would enhance depth and credibility of supporting documents.
